title: Psalms 46
subtitle: God Is Our Refuge and Strength

heb.paragraph: "God is our refuge (maḥăseh) and strength, an ever-present help in trouble" (v.1). Luther’s psalm — the inspiration for "A Mighty Fortress Is Our God." The scenarios of total collapse: earth gives way, mountains fall into the sea, waters roar and foam, mountains quake (vv.2–3). Yet: "God is within her, she will not fall" (v.5). A river brings joy to the city of God (v.4). The city stands because God is in it.

notes.note: "God is our refuge and strength, an ever-present help in trouble." The most quoted verse of confidence in the Psalter. Three identities: refuge (shelter), strength (power), ever-present help (availability).

"Therefore we will not fear, though the earth give way and the mountains fall into the heart of the sea." The worst imaginable scenarios: geological, oceanic, tectonic. None produces fear because God is present.

"There is a river whose streams make glad the city of God. God is within her, she will not fall." Jerusalem had no river — this is a theological river: God’s presence as life-giving water within the city.

refs.note: Isaiah's 'God is with us' (Immanuel) parallels the psalm's refrain 'the LORD Almighty is with us.'
The Emmanuel prophecy fulfilled in Christ's birth embodies the psalm's confidence in divine presence.
The shaking of heavens and earth preceding the unshakable kingdom echoes the psalm's cosmic imagery.

hist.context: Psalm 46 is the first of the 'Songs of Zion' in the Korahite collection, celebrating Jerusalem's inviolability. The 'city of God' with its river (v.4) evokes Eden's rivers and the eschatological stream from the temple (Ezek 47). Historically, the psalm may commemorate Sennacherib's failed siege of Jerusalem in 701 BC, when the Assyrian army was mysteriously destroyed overnight.

sections.header: Verses 7–11 — “Be Still, and Know That I Am God”

heb.gloss: be still / cease striving / let go
heb.paragraph: "Be still (harpū), and know that I am God; I will be exalted among the nations, I will be exalted in the earth" (v.10). The psalm’s climax. Harpū means "stop fighting," "let go of your weapons," "cease your own efforts." It is a command to surrender control, not merely to be quiet. The refrain: "The LORD Almighty is with us; the God of Jacob is our fortress" (vv.7, 11). God’s title "LORD Almighty" (YHWH ṣəbāʾōṭ) — commander of heaven’s armies.

notes.note: "Come and see what the LORD has done. He makes wars cease to the ends of the earth. He breaks the bow and shatters the spear; he burns the shields with fire." God’s peace is not negotiated but imposed. He destroys the weapons themselves.
"Be still, and know that I am God." Not a whisper but a command. Stop striving. Stop managing. Stop fighting. Know that I am God.
"The LORD Almighty is with us; the God of Jacob is our fortress." The refrain. Two names: LORD of armies (cosmic power) + God of Jacob (personal covenant). Both applied to "us."

refs.note: The tabernacle of God with humanity fulfills the psalm's vision of God dwelling in his city.
Beating swords into plowshares parallels the psalm's vision of wars ceasing to the ends of the earth.
The warrior bow cut off and peace proclaimed to the nations extends the psalm's disarmament vision.

hist.context: 'Be still and know that I am God' (v.10) commands cessation from human striving. The Hebrew harpū ('be still') can mean 'let go' or 'cease.' Luther's famous hymn 'A Mighty Fortress Is Our God' paraphrases this psalm. The Selah markings suggest liturgical pauses, and the refrain 'The LORD Almighty is with us' may have been congregational response.

ppl.name: God (Elohim)
ppl.text: The psalm's opening declaration — 'God is our refuge and strength, an ever-present help in trouble' (v.1) — became Martin Luther's inspiration for 'A Mighty Fortress Is Our God.' The divine presence stabilizes the city: 'God is within her, she will not fall; God will help her at break of day' (v.5). The climactic command 'Be still, and know that I am God' (v.10) translates harpu — literally 'let your hands drop,' a military stand-down order. God commands the nations to cease fighting because he will be exalted.

ppl.name: Yahweh Sabaoth / God of Jacob
ppl.text: The refrain uses two titles: 'LORD Almighty' (Yahweh Tsevaot, 'LORD of armies' — cosmic military power) and 'God of Jacob' (personal covenant relationship with a flawed patriarch). This pairing — infinite power joined to intimate care — is the psalm's theological engine. The 'fortress' (misgav) in the refrain is a military high ground, a strategic defensive position, applied metaphorically to God.

ppl.name: The Sons of Korah
ppl.role: Korahite composers of a Zion-confidence psalm (superscription)
ppl.text: This is among the most celebrated of the Korahite psalms — a 'song of Zion' that places God's presence as the city's true fortification. The Alamoth in the superscription may indicate high-pitched voices or a musical term for a specific mode. The psalm's structure moves from natural catastrophe (vv.2-3) through political upheaval (vv.6) to divine resolution (vv.8-10).

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVGod is within her, she will not fall; God will help her at break of day.ESVGod is in the midst of her; she shall not be moved; God will help her when morning dawns.NIVthough its waters roar and foam and the mountains quake with their surging.ESVthough its waters roar and foam, though the mountains tremble at its swelling. Selah

rec.text: ""God is our refuge and strength, an ever-present help in trouble." The most quoted verse of confidence in the Psalter. Three identities: refuge (shelter), strength (power), ever-present help (availabi…"

rows.label: Cosmic Collapse — 46:1–6
rows.text: "Though the earth give way." God is refuge, strength, ever-present. The river gladdens the city.

chiasm.title: Psalm 46 — God Our Refuge: A Mighty Fortress

pairs.label: A
pairs.top: God is our refuge and strength, an ever-present help in trouble. Therefore we will not fear, though the earth give way and the mountains fall into the heart of the sea (vv.1–3)
pairs.bottom: The LORD Almighty is with us; the God of Jacob is our fortress. Come and see what the LORD has done, the desolations he has brought on the earth (vv.8–11)

pairs.label: B
pairs.top: Though the waters roar and foam and the mountains quake with their surging — cosmic chaos threatens but cannot prevail (vv.3–4a)
pairs.bottom: He makes wars cease to the ends of the earth; he breaks the bow and shatters the spear; he burns the shields with fire — human chaos is ended (v.9)

center.label: X
center.text: There is a river whose streams make glad the city of God, the holy place where the Most High dwells. God is within her, she will not fall; God will help her at break of day. Nations are in uproar, kingdoms fall; he lifts his voice, the earth melts (vv.4b–7). The center contrasts the river of God’s peace with the uproar of the nations. While chaos rages outside, the city of God has a quiet river — and when God speaks, it is the earth itself that melts.

hebtext.note: "God is our refuge (maḥăseh) and strength, an ever-present help in trouble" (v.1). Luther’s psalm — the inspiration for "A Mighty Fortress Is Our God." The scenarios of total collapse: earth gives way, mountains fall into the sea, waters roar and foam, mountains quake (vv.2–3). Yet: "God is within her, she will not fall" (v.5). A river brings joy to the city of God (v.4). The city stands because God is in it.

hebtext.gloss: be still / cease striving / let go
hebtext.note: "Be still (harpū), and know that I am God; I will be exalted among the nations, I will be exalted in the earth" (v.10). The psalm’s climax. Harpū means "stop fighting," "let go of your weapons," "cease your own efforts." It is a command to surrender control, not merely to be quiet. The refrain: "The LORD Almighty is with us; the God of Jacob is our fortress" (vv.7, 11). God’s title "LORD Almighty" (YHWH ṣəbāʾōṭ) — commander of heaven’s armies.

themes.note: Sovereignty peaks at 10: God rules over cosmic collapse, nations, warfare. Faith at 9: "be still" is the command to trust. Luther’s psalm.
